title: A Look at Barbarian Prince - A Stone-cold Classic of Solo Adventure Gaming
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0KEJ_zq0Ak

Daniel (@dungeondive) celebrates Barbarian Prince, the 1981 Dwarfstar solo classic — and a stone-cold favourite — now free and legally downloadable (Reaper, the rights-holder, permits distribution). You play Cal Arath, a deposed prince who must raise 500 gold and reclaim his throne within 70 days, roaming a hex map where simply not getting lost is half the battle. He recommends the BGG v2 edition with errata baked in, and shows how little you need: rulebook, paragraph-driven event book, a printed map, and pencil-and-paper tracking.

He plays a vivid sample — crossing the northern river to escape the royal guard, a thieves'-guild heist netting 200 gold, hiring a swordsman, then talking a wealthy dwarf looter into joining for half the loot — demonstrating the loop of travel, get-lost checks, terrain events, feeding the party, and hunting. It's ~99% dice and tables with occasional choices, so it's not for the luck-averse; he's never actually won. But as the ultimate slow-quest sword-and-sorcery game, endlessly replayable and journaling-friendly, it comes very highly recommended — especially at the price of free.

----

Barbarian Prince lives and dies by the dice — Daniel's never won, and getting lost for a week is part of the charm. Do you embrace that kind of high-luck, journal-it-as-you-go solo adventure, or does it frustrate you?
